Data Visualization - Mini-Project 1

Sukhadevsingh Jogindrasingh Virdee svirdee2943@floridapoly.edu

Introduction

Data visualization is a powerful tool for exploring, understanding, and communicating patterns within large datasets. By transforming raw data into graphical representations, analysts can identify trends, relationships, and insights that may not be immediately apparent from tables or summary statistics alone.

This project analyzes the New York City Rat Sightings data set, which contains records of rat sighting complaints reported through the city’s 311 service system. The data set includes information about the location and timing of reported sightings, making it useful for examining geographic and temporal patterns of rodent activity throughout the city.

The primary objective of this analysis is to explore how rat sightings vary across New York City’s boroughs, months of the year, and days of the week. Understanding these patterns can provide insights into environmental conditions, population density, seasonal influences, and reporting behaviors that may contribute to fluctuations in reported sightings.

To accomplish this objective, the data set was cleaned, summarized, and visualized using R and the ggplot2 package. Multiple visualizations were created to highlight key trends and support data-driven interpretations. The findings from this analysis demonstrate how visualization techniques can be used to effectively communicate meaningful patterns in public service and urban environmental data.

Load Libraries

library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(readr)

Load data from data.csv

data <- read_csv("../data/rats_nyc.csv")
head(data)

1. Selected Dataset Description

For this Data Visualization Mini-Project, I selected the New York City (NYC) 311 Rat Sighting Dataset. This dataset tracks public health rodent complaints submitted through the city’s 311 system. It consists detailing the spatial, temporal, and administrative features of every recorded incident. This dataset is appropriate for analysis because it helps identify temporal trends, geographic patterns, and common locations associated with rat sightings across New York City

2. Key Important Variables

To address the primary target business and research questions, the analysis focuses closely on these essential variables:

  • Temporal Dimensions (sighting_year, sighting_month, sighting_day, sighting_weekday): These fields extract specific time stamps from the initial report creation dates. They are critical for understanding cyclical patterns (e.g., assessing if complaints spike over weekends or during warmer summer months) and tracking long-term trends over multiple years.
  • Geographical Identifiers (borough, city, incident_zip): These categorical dimensions allow us to group and aggregate complaints to determine which municipal administrative zones or residential neighborhoods experience the highest density of reports.
  • Physical Context (location_type): This column identifies the category of structures involved (e.g., “1-2 Family Dwelling”, “Commercial Building”, or “3+ Family Mixed Use Building”), helping to isolate what structural environments are most commonly associated with reported infestations.
  • Status Metrics (status): Tracks the progress of the complaint (e.g., “Closed”, “Assigned”, or “Pending”) to gauge operational resolution efficiency.

3. Profiling Missing Values and Data Integrity

A professional data validation pass was conducted on the 41,845 records to understand the completeness of the dataset before visualization:

  • 100% Complete Variables: Core indicators such as unique_key, created_date, agency, borough, and all derived temporal flags (sighting_year, sighting_month, sighting_day, and sighting_weekday) are 100% complete with zero missing values. This ensures reliable aggregation for yearly and daily complaint trend analysis.
  • Partially Missing Operational Metrics: The closed_date variable contains 36,946 non-null records, which means about 4,899 records lack a closing date. This is consistent with the operational context where ongoing or recently assigned cases are naturally marked as pending or open.
  • Partially Missing Spatial Metrics: The latitude, longitude, and precise coordinate variables are missing for 319 observations (with 41,526 non-null rows). These missing geographic records will be excluded from map-based visualizations.
  • Entirely Empty Variables: Several administrative columns—such as facility_type, vehicle_type, and taxi_company_borough— are empty. These are placeholder variables built into the standardized 311 framework but are irrelevant to rodent complaints, and can safely be excluded from our plotting layers.

Visualization 1 - Rat Sightings by Borough

data %>%
  count(borough) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(borough, n), y = n, fill = borough)) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Rat Sightings by Borough",
    x = "Borough",
    y = "Number of Sightings"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Explanation

This bar chart shows the number of rat sightings reported in each New York City borough. The visualization helps identify which boroughs experience the highest number of complaints. Boroughs with higher urban density may report more sightings. Using color and horizontal bars improves readability and makes comparisons easier.

Visualization 2 — Rat Sightings by Month

data %>%
  count(sighting_month) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = sighting_month, y = n)) +
  geom_line(size = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  labs(
    title = "Rat Sightings Throughout the Year",
    x = "Month",
    y = "Number of Sightings"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Explanation

This line chart displays how rat sightings change across different months of the year. The visualization helps reveal seasonal patterns in rat activity. Higher sightings during warmer months may indicate increased outdoor activity and breeding patterns. The use of a line graph is effective because it clearly shows trends over time.

Visualization 3 — Rat Sightings by Weekday

data %>%
  count(sighting_weekday) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(sighting_weekday, n), y = n, fill = sighting_weekday)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  labs(
    title = "Rat Sightings by Weekday",
    x = "Weekday",
    y = "Number of Sightings"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Explanation

This visualization compares the number of rat sightings reported on different days of the week. The chart helps identify whether complaints are more common on weekdays or weekends. Differences may reflect human reporting behavior rather than actual rat activity. Color and ordering improve the clarity of comparisons.

Story Told by the Data

The data reveals important patterns about rat sightings in New York City. Certain boroughs report significantly more sightings than others, suggesting population density and urban conditions may influence rat activity. Seasonal trends indicate that sightings increase during warmer months. Additionally, reporting patterns vary slightly across weekdays, which may reflect differences in public activity and reporting habits.

Design Principles Used

Several visualization principles were applied in this project. Clear titles and axis labels were used to improve readability. Minimal themes reduced unnecessary distractions and kept the focus on the data. Appropriate chart types were selected for different data relationships, such as bar charts for comparisons and line charts for trends over time. Color was also used carefully to distinguish categories and improve interpretation.

Conclusion

This analysis explored rat sighting complaints in New York City and identified meaningful patterns across boroughs, months, and weekdays. The results suggest that densely populated urban areas experience more reported sightings and that complaints increase during warmer months. The visualizations effectively communicate these trends and demonstrate how data visualization can be used to uncover patterns and support data-driven insights.
